figures.gloss: The all-pervading Supreme Being who sustains the multiverse. He dreams the cosmos into existence while slumbering on the serpent of infinity upon the causal ocean, periodically waking to intervene and uphold cosmic order. He is pure Sattva (goodness), infinite, and radiantly sovereign.
figures.facet: Unlike static preservers or distant clockmaker gods, his preservation is deeply active and intimately loving; he does not merely observe from afar but dynamically orchestrates the salvation of souls across infinite cosmic cycles.
figures.provenance: Rigveda (as a solar deity); Shatapatha Brahmana; Bhagavata Purana; Pancharatra Agamas.

figures.gloss: The eighth avatar, worshipped by many lineages as the Supreme Personality ('Svayam Bhagavan') himself. He spans roles from the mischievous butter-thief and enchanting flute-player of Vrindavan to the absolute cosmic sovereign reciting the Bhagavad Gita on the battlefield.
figures.facet: He collapses the infinite distance between God and humanity by actively seeking intimacy through playful, transgressive love ('rasa lila'), establishing emotional devotion as a higher path than rigid Vedic duty.
figures.provenance: Mahabharata (Bhagavad Gita); Bhagavata Purana (Tenth Canto); Harivamsa.

figures.gloss: The seventh avatar and the ultimate exemplar of 'dharma' (righteousness). As the ideal king ('maryada purushottama'), he exiles himself to the forest to honor his father's word and slays the ten-headed demon-king Ravana to rescue his kidnapped wife, Sita.
figures.facet: His divinity is heavily cloaked in agonizing human limitation; he suffers true grief and binds himself strictly to worldly rules, demonstrating that absolute perfection is achieved through unyielding duty rather than miraculous shortcuts.
figures.provenance: Ramayana of Valmiki; Ramcharitmanas of Tulsidas.

figures.gloss: The doctrine of the divine 'crossing down' into material form. Whenever dharma declines and chaos rises, the Supreme takes birth in various forms—fish, turtle, boar, man-lion, or human—to protect the good, annihilate evil, and reestablish cosmic balance.
figures.facet: Unlike prophets who only deliver a message, an avatar is the actual ontological descent of the absolute into finite biology, subjecting itself to mortal physics and karma to manually repair the fabric of reality.
figures.provenance: Bhagavad Gita (4.7-8); Vishnu Purana; Jayadeva's Dashavatara Stotra.

figures.gloss: The eternal consort of Vishnu and the goddess of spiritual and material abundance. In Srivaishnava theology, she acts as the supreme mediatrix ('Purushakara'), transforming Vishnu's strict cosmic justice into infinite, accessible compassion for the devoted soul.
figures.facet: She proves that absolute sovereign power without grace is terrifying to the sinner; divinity requires a mediating maternal matrix of pure forgiveness so that flawed humans can actually approach the absolute.
figures.provenance: Shri Sukta; Vishnu Purana; Pancharatra Agamas; Ramanuja's Sharanagati Gadyam.

figures.gloss: The thousand-headed serpent of infinity floating upon the causal ocean. He serves as Vishnu's divine bed during universal dissolution and physically incarnates alongside Vishnu (as Lakshmana and Balarama) to serve him in the mortal realm.
figures.facet: In a stark reversal of global mythologies where the primordial serpent represents chaos to be slain, Shesha is the ultimate emblem of cosmic stability, servitude, and the remainder that survives when all else is destroyed.
figures.provenance: Mahabharata; Bhagavata Purana; Vishnu Purana.

figures.gloss: The ferocious, liminal man-lion avatar. Manifested from a pillar at twilight to circumvent a demon's boons of invulnerability, he is pure, terrifying grace erupting into the world strictly to protect his absolute devotee, Prahlada.
figures.facet: He embodies the terrifying violence of divine love. His horrific, blood-soaked form is a nightmare to the cosmos but intensely comforting to the devotee, proving God will literally shatter reality's rules to save his own.
figures.provenance: Vishnu Purana; Bhagavata Purana (Canto 7); Narasimha Tapaniya Upanishad.

figures.gloss: The ultimate soteriological path of Vaishnavism: intense, emotional devotion ('bhakti') culminating in total, unconditional surrender ('prapatti') at the lotus feet of the Lord. It explicitly supersedes all rituals, scriptural knowledge, and asceticism as the highest means of liberation.
figures.facet: It fundamentally shifts the burden of salvation from human ascetic effort to the deity's infinite grace; the soul's only true action is to stop resisting the Lord's salvific gravity.
figures.provenance: Bhagavad Gita (Chapter 12, 18.66); Divya Prabandham of the Alvars; Narada Bhakti Sutra.

figures.gloss: The monkey-formed son of the wind-god Vayu whose perfect, fearless service to Rama — crossing the ocean to Lanka, burning the demon city, bearing the mountain of healing herbs — makes him devotion itself in bodily form. He commands an immense independent cult across South and Southeast Asia, worshipped for protection, strength, and unwavering loyalty.
figures.facet: Hanuman shows bhakti carried to its logical extreme: a being whose entire theological identity is servitude, and who is worshipped precisely because he asks nothing for himself.
figures.provenance: Ramayana of Valmiki (Sundara Kanda); Ramcharitmanas of Tulsidas; Hanuman Chalisa.

figures.gloss: Rama's consort, born of the earth (found in a furrow by King Janaka) and worshipped as an avatar of Lakshmi. Abducted by Ravana, she proves her purity by fire (agni pariksha), and at the story's end asks the Earth to reclaim her rather than face a second public trial of her chastity.
figures.facet: Where Rama's divinity is tested by duty, Sita's is tested by suffering and public doubt — the joint cult of Sita-Rama holds up endurance, not conquest, as the other half of righteous kingship.
figures.provenance: Ramayana of Valmiki; Ramcharitmanas of Tulsidas.

figures.gloss: The cowherd woman of Vrindavan whose passionate, extramarital love for Krishna is elevated in Gaudiya and allied Vaishnava lineages to the status of his eternal divine counterpart, worshipped jointly as Radha-Krishna and theologically identified as his hladini-shakti, the very power of divine bliss.
figures.facet: Radha's cult makes transgressive human longing — not lawful marriage — the theological template for the soul's relationship to God, inverting the usual order where divinity sanctifies convention.
figures.provenance: Jayadeva's Gita Govinda; Bhagavata Purana (Tenth Canto, implicitly); Chaitanya Charitamrita of Krishnadasa Kaviraja.

figures.gloss: The great divine eagle, born to devour serpents (nagas) and free his own mother Vinata from bondage to their mother Kadru, who becomes Vishnu's mount and the emblem crowning the flagstaff of virtually every Vaishnava temple.
figures.facet: Garuda's perpetual war with the nagas gives Vishnu's peaceable cosmic order a violent guardian at its gate — the god of preservation still needs something to actively devour the world's dangers.
figures.provenance: Mahabharata (Astika Parva); Garuda Purana; Vishnu Purana.

figures.gloss: The tenth and final avatar, prophesied to appear as a sword-bearing rider on a white horse at the nadir of the Kali Yuga, destroying corruption and the wicked to end the current cosmic age and turn the wheel back to a new Satya Yuga.
figures.facet: Kalki completes the avatara doctrine by pointing it forward rather than back — the only avatar the tradition awaits rather than reveres as already accomplished.
figures.provenance: Vishnu Purana; Bhagavata Purana (Canto 12); Kalki Purana.

figures.gloss: Krishna's elder half-brother, a plough- and club-wielding strongman worshipped as the incarnation of Ananta Shesha and, in several avatara lists, counted an avatar of Vishnu in his own right. He is worshipped beside Krishna and Subhadra at the great Puri temple.
figures.facet: Balarama shows the corpus's cosmic-serpent archetype walking among mortals as a person rather than resting coiled beneath the god he supports — infinity's remainder made flesh and given a plough.
figures.provenance: Mahabharata; Bhagavata Purana (Tenth Canto); Harivamsa.

figures.gloss: The 16th-century Bengali ecstatic (1486-1534) who founded Gaudiya Vaishnavism, making congregational chanting of the divine names (sankirtana) the defining practice of the age; his own followers worship him as a joint, hidden incarnation of Radha and Krishna in one body.
figures.facet: Through the 20th-century ISKCON mission, Chaitanya's specific ecstatic-congregational model — not the temple cult of Vishnu generally — became the form in which Vaishnavism went global.
figures.provenance: Chaitanya Charitamrita of Krishnadasa Kaviraja; Chaitanya Bhagavata of Vrindavan Das.

figures.gloss: Twelve Tamil poet-saints (6th-9th centuries) whose ecstatic devotional hymns to Vishnu are canonized as the Divya Prabandham, the 'Tamil Veda' of Srivaishnavism, given equal scriptural standing with the Sanskrit texts by the tradition's later theologians.
figures.facet: By granting vernacular Tamil poetry the authority of revealed scripture, the Alvars made regional bhakti — not just Sanskrit ritual literacy — a valid path to the same canon.
figures.provenance: Divya Prabandham (compiled by Nathamuni, c. 9th-10th c.); Guruparamparai hagiographical histories of Srivaishnavism.

figures.gloss: Vishnu's eternal abode beyond the material cosmos, where he reigns enthroned with Lakshmi amid liberated souls who have attained his direct service; in most Vaishnava soteriologies, liberation (moksha) is understood not as dissolution of the self but as eternal residence here.
figures.facet: Vaikuntha keeps the personal, relational structure of devotion intact even at the endpoint of salvation — unlike liberation-as-merger doctrines, the devotee remains a devotee forever, only now face to face with the Lord.
figures.provenance: Vishnu Purana; Bhagavata Purana; Pancharatra Agamas.

figures.gloss: Krishna's battlefield discourse to the despairing warrior Arjuna, embedded in the Mahabharata's Bhishma Parva — roughly 700 verses compressing the theology of avatara, disinterested action (nishkama karma), and devotional surrender that half the Vaishnava roster cites as its charter.
figures.facet: The Gita's genius is staging its metaphysics inside a crisis of conscience rather than a cosmogony — theology delivered as counsel to one frightened man rather than as an account of creation.
figures.provenance: Mahabharata, Bhishma Parva, chapters 23-40 (the Bhagavad Gita proper); commentaries of Shankara, Ramanuja, and Madhva.

figures.gloss: The 'Lord of the World,' a great-eyed, stump-armed wooden form of Krishna enshrined at Puri alongside his siblings Balabhadra (Balarama) and Subhadra, whose annual Ratha Yatra draws the deity out through the city on colossal chariots before one of the largest recurring crowds in any living religion.
figures.facet: Jagannath's crude, aniconic wooden form — deliberately unfinished, periodically replaced in a secret rite — makes the god's presence a matter of ongoing communal renewal rather than a single perfected image.
figures.provenance: Puri temple tradition and the Skanda Purana's Puri-related sections; the Daru Brahma (sacred-wood) legend of the image's origin.

figures.gloss: The basil plant venerated as a goddess and devotee-consort of Vishnu, grown in a ritual pot (tulasi vrindavan) at the center of Vaishnava households; an offering to Vishnu made without her leaf is held incomplete. Her annual ritual marriage to Vishnu (Tulasi Vivaha) opens the Hindu wedding season.
figures.facet: Tulasi keeps Vaishnava devotion tethered to a literal, growing, daily-tended plant rather than only to image or myth — the god's worship runs, in practice, through a garden.
figures.provenance: Padma Purana; Brahma Vaivarta Purana; Tulasi Vivaha household ritual tradition.

figures.gloss: The 11th-12th-century (c. 1017-1137) South Indian theologian who systematized Srivaishnavism's Vishishtadvaita ('qualified non-dualism') philosophy, folding the Alvars' Tamil hymns and the doctrine of Lakshmi's mediating grace (Purushakara) into a rigorous Sanskrit theology.
figures.facet: Ramanuja is the hinge between ecstatic vernacular bhakti and institutional Vaishnava philosophy, giving the Alvars' poetry a systematic doctrinal skeleton it did not originally carry.
figures.provenance: Ramanuja's Sri Bhashya (commentary on the Brahma Sutras) and Sharanagati Gadyam; Srivaishnava guruparamparai histories.

figures.gloss: The one woman among the twelve Alvars, an 8th-9th-century Tamil poet-saint whose Tiruppavai and Nachiyar Tirumozhi describe her insistence on marrying Vishnu alone; tradition holds she was ritually wed to his image at Srirangam and absorbed into it, and she is worshipped today as a goddess in her own right.
figures.facet: Andal's cult turns a single poet's refusal of any human marriage into a permanent theological fact — devotion so total it is said to have physically ended in union with the god.
figures.provenance: Tiruppavai and Nachiyar Tirumozhi (attributed to Andal); Srivaishnava guruparamparai hagiography.

figures.gloss: Vishnu's spinning, serrated discus weapon, said to have 108 blades and to return to his hand after severing any enemy of dharma; in some Vaishnava traditions it is invoked and worshipped as a fierce deity in its own right, distinct from Vishnu's peaceful iconography.
figures.facet: The Chakra is the violent instrument the preserving god keeps in reserve — proof that Vishnu's preservation of cosmic order is backed by force, not benevolence alone.
figures.provenance: Mahabharata; Vishnu Purana; Sudarshana Samhita of the Pancharatra Agamas.

figures.gloss: The boar avatar who plunges into the cosmic ocean to rescue the earth-goddess Bhudevi after the demon Hiranyaksha drags her to the depths, raising her on his tusks and resettling her upon the waters.
figures.facet: Varaha gives the corpus's earth-diver pattern its Vaishnava register: not a trickster animal fetching mud for a new creation, but the Supreme Being himself diving to rescue a creation already made.
figures.provenance: Vishnu Purana; Bhagavata Purana (Canto 3); Varaha Purana.

figures.gloss: The dwarf avatar who approaches the demon-king Bali as a humble beggar asking only for as much land as he can cover in three strides, then expands to cosmic size (as Trivikrama) and reclaims heaven, earth, and the underworld in three steps, granting Bali sovereignty over the last as a consolation.
figures.facet: Vamana's disguise makes the avatar doctrine's central move explicit — the Supreme approaches the powerful looking powerless, and wins by growth rather than by force.
figures.provenance: Vishnu Purana; Bhagavata Purana (Canto 8); Shatapatha Brahmana (earliest Vedic seed of the three-strides motif).

figures.gloss: The axe-wielding warrior-sage avatar, a Brahmin by birth who takes up arms to exterminate a corrupt, oppressive kshatriya (warrior) class across the earth twenty-one times over, restoring the proper order between priestly and royal power.
figures.facet: As the only avatar whose active career overlaps with Rama's and Krishna's own lifetimes in the epics, Parashurama shows the avatara doctrine's own timeline is looser than a strict one-avatar-per-age scheme.
figures.provenance: Mahabharata; Bhagavata Purana (Canto 9); Kalki Purana (his eventual role as Kalki's guru).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The indivisible dyad of absolute sovereign reality and its mediating, compassionate grace.
Intra-tradition bond: The unmanifest absolute and its cyclical, localized historical interventions.
Intra-tradition bond: The playful, rule-bending deity of ecstatic love paired against the rigid, suffering exemplar of perfect duty.
Intra-tradition bond: The preserver and the bedrock of infinity upon which he rests and dreams.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The historical evolution of the Vedic sun god's world-encompassing strides into the all-pervading Supreme Being.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The deliberate theological absorption of the Vedic storm king's role as the supreme demon-conqueror and protector of the worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The philosophical mapping of Lakshmi onto Prakriti, identifying her as the active, material energy through which the passive supreme consciousness acts.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The profound structural theological commitment that the absolute must take on finite, suffering flesh to save a broken creation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The manifestation of the absolute truth into a localized, phenomenal body (Nirmanakaya) for the specific soteriological benefit of trapped beings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine feminine emanation that mediates between the unapproachable, ineffable absolute and the material cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The intensely devoted divine mother and consort who actively secures the throne and administers the power of the high god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The alluring, transgressive lord of ecstasy who violently shatters societal norms and rigidity to offer liberation through divine intoxication and dance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The eternally youthful, mesmerizing, and dangerously unpredictable supreme lord who playfully manipulates human destinies like a cosmic game.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The immense, primordial serpent intrinsically bound to cosmic waters, continuous cyclical time, and the underlying stability of the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The soteriological pivot away from grueling ascetic self-effort and toward pure, emotional surrender to an entity of infinite compassion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dutiful, weapon-wielding hero who systematically cleanses the earth of alien, chaos-bringing monsters to make it habitable for humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmic sovereign who calmly dreams or speaks the intricate order of the universe into existence from a state of serene repose.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conceptual absolute of cosmic, social, and personal order ('dharma') made physically manifest in the person and behavior of the perfect ruler.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The theological premise that the individual soul inherently recognizes its exile and burns with a desperate, emotional yearning to reunite with its true source.
GOLD SEAM: the same name, the avatar emptied — Kabir keeps 'Ram' while denying in his own verse that his Ram was ever born or wore a body; nirguna bhakti defined against the saguna avatar.

GOLD SEAM (held low): hagiography from Nabhadas's Bhaktamal (c. 1600) makes Kabir a disciple of the Vaishnava Ramananda, pulling him into the bhakti lineage — the chronology is strained and scholars doubt the link, so the tier stays at claim-strength.
Akilam names this figure directly as Vishnu/Narayana, carrying the Vaishnava supreme deity straight into Ayyavazhi cosmology as Vaikundar's source and commissioner.
A deity petitioned in seasonal domestic ritual specifically to secure the year's material prosperity, welcomed into the home at a fixed calendrical moment.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a goddess of prosperity invoked communally to secure the coming year's abundance and welfare — a household devotional cult on one side, a clan-wide seasonal festival on the other.

the Ginans' 'Das Avatar' tradition explicitly casts 'Ali and the line of Imams as the tenth and final avatar of Vishnu awaited to come — a documented, deliberate scriptural syncretism, not mere structural resemblance, that let Hindu converts recognize Ismaili doctrine inside their own cosmology.

Attested within Jain scripture itself: the Harivaṃśa Purāṇa places Neminātha and Krishna as cousins in one shared Yadava genealogy, folding the Krishna cycle into Jain sacred history rather than merely echoing it.
a goddess of prosperity invoked at the threshold of commerce, her favor secured by daily small offerings rather than temple ritual.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the Nayanars and the Vaishnava Alvars are attested contemporaries of the same Tamil bhakti movement (6th-9th century), parallel vernacular devotional currents to rival Sanskritic gods.
Intra-tradition: the jointly worshipped royal pair, Sita-Rama, ideal king and ideal queen-consort.
Intra-tradition: the avatar-king and his devotee-servant, whose total surrender embodies bhakti in narrative form.
Intra-tradition: the jointly worshipped divine pair, Radha-Krishna, Gaudiya theology's supreme dyad.
Intra-tradition: the brothers of Vrindavan and Dwaraka, worshipped side by side at Puri.
Intra-tradition: the serpent of infinity's own incarnation, resolving the roster's prior unlinked reference to Shesha appearing 'as Lakshmana and Balarama.'
Intra-tradition: the Supreme Being and his mount and battle-standard, crowning nearly every Vaishnava temple flagstaff.
Intra-tradition: the preserver and the weapon that enforces his preservation of dharma by force.
Intra-tradition: the Supreme Being and the eternal abode where liberated souls join him in service.
Intra-tradition: the god and the devotee-consort plant whose leaf makes an offering to him complete.
Intra-tradition: the doctrine of divine descent completed by its one still-awaited instance.
Intra-tradition: the third of the ten classical avatars, the earth-rescuing boar.
Intra-tradition: the fifth of the ten classical avatars, the dwarf who reclaims the cosmos in three strides.
Intra-tradition: the sixth of the ten classical avatars, restorer of Brahmin-kshatriya order.
Intra-tradition: the scripture is Krishna's own recorded discourse.
Intra-tradition: the Gita (4.7-8) is the doctrine of avatara's own scriptural charter, already cited as such in the roster's avatara entry.
Intra-tradition: Jagannath is the localized Puri form of Krishna, worshipped with the same Tenth Canto mythos behind it.
Intra-tradition: Chaitanya is the historical founder who made congregational sankirtana the practical form of the bhakti doctrine already in the roster.
Intra-tradition: the Alvars' Divya Prabandham is already cited as the bhakti entry's own provenance; this restores the poets themselves to the roster.
Intra-tradition: Andal is the one woman counted among the twelve Alvars, later worshipped as a goddess in her own right.
Intra-tradition: Ramanuja's Sharanagati Gadyam (already cited as Lakshmi's provenance) systematizes her role as Purushakara, the mediatrix between the sinner and Vishnu.
(structural only): an earth-born consort abducted, tested, and finally returned to the earth she came from — no descent claimed between the traditions.
the great sky-bird whose natural enemy is the serpent or underwater monster, a widespread structural pairing independently arrived at in South Asia and the Great Lakes.
the awaited restorer who arrives at history's worst point to defeat evil and end the current age, held at structural tier given Zoroastrian and Vaishnava eschatology's independent development.
ecstatic mystics who made public, danced or chanted devotional performance — sankirtana, sama — the highest practice of the age, independently of each other.
the earth-diver pattern shared across unrelated traditions — a being plunges into the primal deep and raises the land upon it, here a god's own avatar rather than a trickster animal.

One deity split across two shards of this corpus by design: this entry is his minor Rigvedic three-strider form, while the vaishnava shard holds the Puranic supreme god he becomes — the roster gap this entry closes.

Same deity, inverted theological rank: mainstream Vaishnavism holds Kṛṣṇa as Viṣṇu's avatāra, while ISKCON's Gauḍīya doctrine makes him svayaṃ bhagavān, the original Godhead of whom Viṣṇu is the expansion.

dossier.essence: One of the principal streams of Hinduism, comprising the traditions that hold Vishnu, together with his descents (avatars) such as Krishna and Rama, to be the supreme personal God. It counts hundreds of millions of adherents, overwhelmingly in South Asia and its diaspora, and spans philosophical schools (Ramanuja's Vishishtadvaita, Madhva's Dvaita) and devotional lineages (sampradayas), from the Tamil Alvars and the Sri Vaishnavas to the Krishna-centered Gaudiya tradition of Chaitanya.
dossier.cosmos: Vishnu is the all-pervading sustainer who periodically descends as an avatar to restore dharma whenever cosmic and moral order decay. Time runs in vast recurring cycles (yugas, kalpas) through which this drama of decline and intervention repeats. The soul (jiva) is real and, though bound in rebirth (samsara) by karma, is destined through grace and devotion for liberation (moksha), most often conceived as loving nearness to God in his heaven (Vaikuntha) rather than impersonal absorption.
dossier.practice: The heart of practice is bhakti, loving devotion, expressed through temple worship and darshan of the enshrined image, pilgrimage, festival, name-chanting (japa, kirtan), and the hearing of sacred story; the Sri Vaishnava path culminates in prapatti, total self-surrender. Life is organized through temples, monastic teacher-lineages (acharyas, gurus), and initiation into a sampradaya; ethics stress devotion, humility, and, in many lineages, vegetarianism and nonviolence.
dossier.canon: It draws on the Vedas and Upanishads but centers on the Bhagavad Gita, the Vishnu and especially the Bhagavata Purana, the Ramayana, the Pancharatra Agamas, and the Tamil hymns of the Alvars (the Nalayira Divya Prabandham).
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the conviction that the absolute is personal and loving, salvation reached less by knowledge or works than by devotion and grace, with God willing to take flesh to reach the devotee.
sources: Friedhelm Hardy, 'Viraha-Bhakti: The Early History of Krsna Devotion in South India' (1983)
John B. Carman, 'The Theology of Ramanuja: An Essay in Interreligious Understanding' (1974)
